es.example: Prometieron una reforma enorme: mucho ruido y pocas nueces.
es.exampleTranslation: They promised a huge reform — all sound and fury, nothing behind it.

it.example: Il suo piano è tutto fumo e niente arrosto.
it.exampleTranslation: His plan is all smoke, no roast.

bridge.note: Both call the bluff with a sense that promises and a substance that never arrives — but they pick different senses. Spanish is auditory: a great rattling of the sack, and hardly any nuts inside. Italian is olfactory: the smoke of a roast rising from a kitchen where no meat is cooking. Hearing versus smelling, and in both cases the appetite is the point.

interference.es_to_it: 'Molto rumore e poche noci' is a translation, not the idiom — Italian smells it: tutto fumo e niente arrosto.
interference.it_to_es: 'Todo humo y nada de asado' is not Spanish; the sack of nuts does the work.

review.notes: From Joe's Italian proverb list 2026-07-18 (learner-site discovery; all glosses and examples authored fresh). ES counterpart chosen by Claude from recall — UNVERIFIED. The ES side is also the Spanish title of Shakespeare's Much Ado — reviewer may want that as a footnote.
